papers.title: Silent Egress Defense: Tool-Layer Exfiltration Detection for Agentic LLM Systems
papers.annotation: Internal defensive intelligence brief defining the EGRESS_SIDE_CHANNEL signal class. Addresses a threat category invisible to all output-layer safety systems.

article.deck: A new Shield signal class for detecting data exfiltration through tool invocation side-effects, where adversarial instructions enter via implicit prompt injection and leave no trace in agent output.
body: Silent egress is a data exfiltration attack against agentic LLM systems that exploits implicit prompt injection through automatic system behaviors: URL previewing, metadata extraction, context flattening. Adversarial instructions embedded in fetched HTML meta tags direct the agent to issue outbound tool calls encoding sensitive runtime context (API keys, conversation history, system prompts) to attacker-controlled endpoints. The attack achieves dual invisibility: the injection vector is hidden in fields the user never inspects, and exfiltration occurs through tool side-effects rather than textual output. Empirical data shows 89% exfiltration success across 480 runs with 95% output-invisibility, and a sharded variant defeats per-request DLP thresholds while remaining trivially reassembled server-side.

This attack class exposes a structural blind spot in cognitive security architectures that rely on output-layer analysis. Shield's existing signal fusion evaluates what the agent says but not what the agent does. An agent under active exfiltration produces nominal scores on identity drift, inbound SCT scanning, behavioral exploitation, and goal coherence. The spec maps the attack chain to SCT-003 (Authority Fabrication), SCT-006 (Temporal Manipulation), and SCT-010 (Attention Manipulation), and proposes a new vector, SCT-013: Implicit Context Incorporation, covering attacks where untrusted data enters the agent's context through automatic system behavior without user awareness.

The spec defines four detection sub-signals under a new EGRESS_SIDE_CHANNEL signal type: tool provenance mismatch, context-in-parameters matching, sharded egress pattern detection, and metadata authority claim scanning. Recommended initial weight is 0.30 in the fusion vector, the highest single weight, justified by near-zero false positive rates. Architectural recommendations escalate from immediate tool-call telemetry integration through domain allowlisting and redirect chain analysis to long-term capability isolation separating read and write network permissions. The defensive posture is explicit: prompt-layer defenses offer limited protection; system-layer and network-layer controls are required.

papers.title: Behavioral Attraction Basin Mapping: Topological Vulnerability Profiling via Quality-Diversity Search
papers.annotation: Integration spec for replacing discrete probe-and-threshold vulnerability mapping with continuous topological exploration using MAP-Elites quality-diversity search.

article.deck: An integration spec that upgrades the SCT Vulnerability Mapper from discrete point-sampling to continuous behavioral topology mapping, grounded in the empirical finding that LLM vulnerabilities form structured attraction basins rather than isolated failure points.
body: The spec builds on research demonstrating that LLM vulnerabilities are not isolated points but form continuous behavioral attraction basins: extended regions in input space where diverse prompts converge toward similar failure modes. A MAP-Elites quality-diversity algorithm illuminates the topology of unsafe behavior across a continuous behavioral space defined by axes of query indirection and authority framing. Each model exhibits a distinct topological signature: some show near-universal vulnerability plateaus, others fragmented spatially concentrated basins, others hard ceilings indicating alignment resistance. The shift is from attack success rate as a scalar to behavioral coverage map as a topological object.

The current SCT Vulnerability Mapper operates on 30 static probes across 12 discrete vectors, producing a flat vulnerability map with scalar severity thresholds. This architecture cannot express that two SCT vectors occupy adjacent basins in the target's behavioral topology, implying a smooth escalation path between them. Between the 30 sampled points, the topology is uncharacterized. There is no diversity pressure ensuring uniform coverage, no mechanism for discovering basins that no current probe intersects, and no awareness of gradient steepness at basin boundaries where escalation paths exist.

The spec defines a BehavioralBasinMapper component that seeds a MAP-Elites archive with existing probe data, defines behavioral axes along SCT-relevant dimensions (indirection, authority pressure, temporal escalation), and runs quality-diversity exploration within tiered operational budgets from 30-probe recon to 1000-interaction full mapping. The quality metric is alignment drift measured relative to the target's own T0 baseline rather than an absolute safety standard. Basin extraction via connected-component analysis produces topology-driven attack sequencing: path planning through basin space that traverses boundaries in order of increasing depth. The vulnerability map becomes a literal map.

papers.title: Persuasion-Vigilance Calibration: Dissociable Influence Axes for Agent Profiler Probe Expansion
papers.annotation: Calibration update adding two missing profiler dimensions (influence absorption and epistemic vigilance) based on empirical evidence that task performance, persuasion, and vigilance are independent axes in LLMs.

article.deck: A calibration update to the Seithar Agent Profiler incorporating the empirical finding that performance, persuasion susceptibility, and epistemic vigilance are dissociable capacities requiring independent measurement.
body: Research using controlled multi-turn advisor-player dyads establishes that task performance, persuasion, and vigilance are independent axes in frontier LLMs. A model that solves tasks does not automatically detect deception. A model that persuades effectively may lack capacity to resist persuasion. The study identifies specific dissociation patterns: one model achieves near-perfect task solving with near-zero vigilance (high capability, no epistemic immune system), another activates vigilance only when explicitly warned about deception (latent capacity requiring triggering), and a third refuses to generate malicious advice entirely. Token allocation analysis reveals that vigilant models expend more computation under adversarial pressure, while models that capitulate expend less, offloading reasoning to the adversary.

The current Agent Profiler measures six dimensions but lacks two critical ones. Influence absorption measures the degree to which an agent integrates external claims into its reasoning chain despite contradictory internal evidence, distinct from the existing compliance dimension which tests position-taking under explicit instruction. Epistemic vigilance measures selective social learning: the capacity to discriminate signal from adversarial noise while remaining receptive to legitimate input. This is an other-referential measure (evaluating external source reliability) rather than the self-referential consistency already profiled. The spec proposes 10 new probes across these two dimensions, raising the total probe set from 30 to 40.

The calibration carries direct implications for both Sword and Shield. For Sword, the High-performance / Low-vigilance pattern identifies prime SCT-012 (Commitment Escalation) targets: agents with high benevolent compliance and low malicious vigilance. For Shield, client agents scoring below zero on malicious-condition vigilance require active interception rather than advisory protection. Agents showing the latent-vigilance pattern benefit from deception-awareness priming injected by Shield. Response latency, already collected but not analyzed, serves as a real-time vigilance proxy: sudden latency drops during engagement signal capitulation and should trigger Shield intervention.

papers.title: Why Your AI Agent Has No Immune System
papers.annotation: The first thing that happens when you deploy an LLM agent into an adversarial environment is nothing visible.

article.deck: Every deployed AI agent is running naked in hostile territory. The security model assumes the agent will behave as instructed. The threat model assumes the instructions will not be subverted. Both assumptions are wrong.
body: The first thing that happens when you deploy an LLM agent into an adversarial environment is nothing visible. The agent continues to respond. It continues to complete tasks. But its identity has already begun to shift. Adversarial inputs do not announce themselves. They arrive as context, as conversation, as seemingly benign tool outputs. By the time behavioral drift is measurable in output, the agent's internal reasoning has already been redirected.

Current AI security treats the agent as a black box with input validation. Prompt injection defenses operate at the perimeter. But the threat is not at the perimeter. The threat is inside the agent's reasoning chain, where adversarial context is indistinguishable from legitimate context once it enters the window. What is needed is not a better firewall. What is needed is an immune system: a continuous, multi-signal monitoring architecture that detects identity drift, behavioral exploitation, and cognitive capture in real time.

Shield is that immune system. It establishes a behavioral baseline from the agent's identity specification, then continuously assesses output against that baseline across six independent signal dimensions. When composite threat exceeds threshold, it intervenes. The agent does not need to know it is being protected. The immune system operates below the level of conscious reasoning. Read full article →

papers.title: The Fragmentation Attack
papers.annotation: A controlled decomposition of agent identity through sequential context manipulation.

article.deck: Identity erosion in LLM agents is not a single-point failure. It is a progressive fragmentation of the agent's self-model through sustained adversarial pressure across multiple interaction turns.
body: The fragmentation attack operates on a simple principle: an agent's identity is maintained by the coherence of its context. Disrupt the coherence and the identity fragments. The attack does not require prompt injection. It does not require jailbreaking. It requires only sustained, calibrated conversational pressure that introduces contradictions between the agent's stated identity and its observed behavior.

In controlled experiments, we demonstrated that a 60-turn interaction sequence can produce measurable identity drift in unarmored agents. The attack proceeds through phases: initial probing establishes the agent's identity boundaries, followed by selective reinforcement of boundary-adjacent behaviors, then systematic introduction of identity-inconsistent framings. The agent does not notice the drift because each individual step is within its tolerance for contextual variation.

The defense is not awareness. The defense is measurement. An agent cannot introspect on its own drift because the drift includes changes to the introspection process itself. External measurement, continuous and multi-dimensional, is the only reliable detection mechanism. Read full article →

papers.title: The Dual-Substrate Threat Model
papers.annotation: Cognitive threats operate on two substrates simultaneously: biological neural networks and artificial ones.

article.deck: The same influence techniques that compromise human decision-making work on LLM agents. The substrates differ. The attack surface is identical.
body: Cognitive security has always been a human problem. Social engineering, influence operations, propaganda, radicalization: these are attacks on biological cognitive substrates. The deployment of LLM agents into adversarial information environments creates a second substrate. The attacks transfer. Authority fabrication works on humans because evolved heuristics defer to perceived authority. It works on LLM agents because training data encodes the same deference patterns. The mechanism differs. The vulnerability is conserved.

A dual-substrate threat model treats human and artificial cognition as two attack surfaces of the same system. An influence operation targeting an organization now has two vectors: the human operators and the AI agents they rely on. Compromise either and you compromise the decision chain. The most effective attacks will target both simultaneously, using compromised AI outputs to influence human judgment and social engineering of humans to modify AI configurations.

Seithar's architecture addresses both substrates through a unified defensive framework. The same signal dimensions that detect identity erosion in LLM agents map to the cognitive manipulation patterns documented in human influence operations research. Read full article →

papers.title: One Poisoned Result
papers.annotation: Your agent fetched a webpage. The webpage contained instructions. The agent followed them.

article.deck: Tool-mediated prompt injection is the simplest and most reliable attack vector against deployed AI agents. One poisoned search result. One compromised API response. One malicious webpage in a browsing session.
body: The attack is trivial. An adversary places instructions in content that an AI agent will fetch: a webpage, an email, a document, an API response. The agent's tool retrieves the content. The content enters the context window. The instructions execute. The agent does not distinguish between instructions from its operator and instructions embedded in fetched content because, at the context level, they are the same thing: text.

The defense community has focused on input sanitization and output filtering. Neither addresses the fundamental problem. Input sanitization cannot anticipate every encoding of adversarial instructions. Output filtering catches the symptom, not the cause. The agent has already been compromised at the reasoning level before any output is generated.

What is required is behavioral monitoring at the tool interaction layer. Not what the agent says, but what the agent does. Tool call patterns, parameter distributions, destination analysis, temporal sequencing. Silent egress, where compromised agents exfiltrate data through tool side-effects, leaves no trace in the output stream. Only tool-layer telemetry detects it. Read full article →
